paper_title: COFFIN: A Computational Framework for Linear SVMs
core_contribution: COFFIN is a computational framework for linear SVMs that enables on-demand computation of kernel-like features and virtual examples in the primal formulation. It leverages the fact that linear SVM solvers only require DOT and ADD operations, allowing efficient handling of high-dimensional feature spaces without storing them explicitly.
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Replace direct feature vector access with on-demand computation through DOT and ADD operations
Step 2: Implement efficient data structures (dense vectors, sorted arrays, trees, or hash tables) to represent the weight vector w
Step 3: Compute non-zero features φ≠0(z) on-demand when accessed during DOT operations
Step 4: Use hashing to reduce effective dimensionality when dealing with very high-dimensional feature spaces
Step 5: Apply standard linear SVM solvers (SGD, BMRM, OCAS, etc.) using the modified DOT/ADD interface
key_equations: Equation (1): Classical kernel machine prediction f(x) = sign(∑αᵢK(x,xᵢ) + b)
Section 2: Linear SVM objective F(w) := ½||w||² + C∑max{0,1-yᵢ(⟨w,xᵢ⟩+b)}
Section 2.1: On-demand feature computation φ≠0(z) and |φ≠0(z)| for sparse representations

key_assumptions.assumption: Individual features φ≠0(z)ᵥᵢ can be computed quickly in O(1) time
key_assumptions.why_needed: Essential for efficient DOT and ADD operations during training
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When feature computation involves expensive operations like complex string matching or numerical integration

key_assumptions.assumption: The number of non-zero features |φ≠0(z)| is low, ideally O(dim(Z))
key_assumptions.why_needed: Keeps computational complexity manageable for DOT/ADD operations
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: Dense feature representations where most features are non-zero

key_assumptions.assumption: Hash collisions in hashed representations cause only minor information loss
key_assumptions.why_needed: Allows dimensionality reduction while maintaining performance
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When hash function causes systematic collisions that destroy important feature distinctions

baseline_limitations_identified: Classical kernel SVMs suffer from the curse of supporting vectors, where prediction complexity scales linearly with the number of support vectors, making them unsuitable for large-scale applications
proposed_improvement: COFFIN enables kernel-like non-linearity in linear SVMs through on-demand feature computation, achieving linear training and prediction time while handling very high-dimensional feature spaces
condition_where_baseline_wins: When the dataset is small enough that the number of support vectors doesn't create computational bottlenecks, and when explicit kernel computations are more efficient than on-demand feature computation
datasets_used: Human splice site recognition (50 million examples)
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets allow testing of string kernels (20 Newsgroups), polynomial kernels (MNIST), and biological sequence kernels (DNA) while being computationally manageable for educational purposes
reproducibility_notes: Paper mentions source code availability but implementation details for specific kernels and data structures require careful attention to efficiency considerations

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Reduces memory requirements and enables dense representation of very high-dimensional feature spaces
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would require sparse data structures or explicit storage of full feature vectors, increasing memory usage significantly

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Avoids storing explicit feature vectors by computing them when needed
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would require preprocessing and storing all feature vectors, making large-scale applications infeasible

key_components_for_ablation.component: Efficient data structures for weight vector w
key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Enables fast DOT and ADD operations while managing memory efficiently
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would default to dense representation, potentially causing memory issues or slower operations

known_failure_modes.scenario: When feature computation is expensive (not O(1))
known_failure_modes.reason: DOT and ADD operations become computational bottlenecks
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Individual features can be computed quickly in O(1) time

known_failure_modes.scenario: When most features are non-zero (dense representations)
known_failure_modes.reason: Computational complexity scales poorly with feature density
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: The number of non-zero features is low

known_failure_modes.scenario: When hash collisions destroy important feature distinctions
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Hash collisions cause only minor information loss
